representative_text: You fainted, but our evaluation shows no dangerous cause.
Chest pain not caused by your heart — tests showed your heart is healthy.
You likely have BPPV, a problem with tiny crystals in your inner ear that makes you dizzy when you move your head.
Abdominal pain with no concerning cause found in the emergency department.
You had a skin infection that formed a pocket of pus, which we drained today.
You had an allergic reaction that is now better.
You have a bladder infection (also called a urinary tract infection or UTI).
Based on everything we found, this fainting episode was likely caused by dehydration, standing up too quickly, or a temporary drop in blood flow to your brain — not a heart or brain problem.
Based on your exam and these results, your chest pain is likely from muscle strain, stress, or irritation of the chest wall, not from blocked arteries or a heart attack.
We also checked your blood and urine to make sure you did not have dangerous complications from high blood sugar.
Most sinus infections are caused by viruses, so antibiotics won't help—your body will fight this off on its own over the next 7-10 days.
All tests came back normal — your heart muscle is not damaged and your heart rhythm is regular.
The bite looks clean without spreading redness (called erythema migrans).
The pain happens when the stone moves through the narrow tube from your kidney to your bladder.
The ultrasound showed gallstones in your gallbladder.
We checked your vision, examined your eyes carefully, and looked for signs of a more serious problem.
Based on these findings, you do not have a dangerous heart problem right now.
Avoid hot showers or baths for 24 hours — heat can bring hives back.
Add extra salt to your meals for the next few days unless you have high blood pressure
Avoid dehydration — drink extra water if you exercise or sweat.
After 2-3 days, start gentle wrist movements — slow circles and bending up and down — if pain allows.
Get 7-8 hours of sleep each night
Avoid alcohol, which can make itching worse
Apply a heating pad on low setting for 15-20 minutes at a time to sore areas, up to 3 times a day
Avoid alcohol completely until your symptoms are gone
**Wrap your ankle** — use an elastic bandage (ACE wrap) from your toes to mid-calf. Keep it snug but not so tight that your toes turn blue or numb. Rewrap it if it loosens.
Wrap your knee with an elastic bandage (ACE wrap) snugly but not so tight that your toes tingle or turn blue
Eat small, easy meals when you feel hungry — soups and broths work well.
Apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment (like bacitracin or Neosporin) twice daily and cover with a clean bandage
Apply a heating pad to your right upper belly for 15-20 minutes if you have mild discomfort
Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for shortness of breath or wheezing — use 15-30 minutes before exercise. May cause fast heartbeat or shakiness.
**Dextromethorphan cough syrup** 10-20 mg (follow bottle instructions) every 4-6 hours as needed for cough — avoid if you take certain antidepressants (check with your pharmacist).
May cause nausea or change urine color to dark yellow or brown (this is normal).
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days — take with food to prevent stomach upset. Watch for diarrhea or rash.
No new medications. Continue your home medications as before.
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for fever or body aches — take with food or milk. Do not take more than 4000 mg in 24 hours. Watch for nausea if you take too much.
Ibuprofen 400 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for pain — take with food. Watch for stomach upset.
Acetaminophen 650 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for pain if you cannot take ibuprofen — do not take more than 3000 mg in 24 hours. Watch for using too much, which can hurt your liver.

Erythromycin eye ointment: apply a thin ribbon to the inside of your lower eyelid 4 times daily for 3-5 days — this prevents infection while the scratch heals. Your vision may be blurry for 20 minutes after each use.
Do not use if your burning improves with the antibiotic.
Continue your daily controller inhaler (if you have one) exactly as prescribed — do not stop it.
Dizziness, fainting, or feeling like you might pass out.
Chest pain gets worse or does not go away with rest
You cannot keep down liquids for more than 12 hours
The bitten area becomes more painful instead of better after 2 days
Sudden severe headache that feels like the worst headache of your life
You develop fever over 100.4°F
You need your rescue inhaler more than every 4 hours
**Severe headache with stiff neck** or confusion.
Shortness of breath at rest
You have a severe headache that starts suddenly

Call your primary care doctor if your symptoms do not improve within 5-7 days. Most viral sore throats get better on their own in 5-10 days. You do not need an urgent appointment unless your symptoms worsen.
Bring your home blood pressure log with you.
See your primary care doctor within 5-7 days.
Call their office tomorrow to schedule an appointment.
Call sooner if you need help identifying what caused the rash or if symptoms worsen.
Your doctor may refer you to a urologist (kidney and bladder specialist) to prevent future stones, especially if this is not your first stone.
Call sooner if you need a work note, physical therapy referral, or stronger pain medicine.
If symptoms get much better before then, routine follow-up at your next regular visit is fine.
If you do not have a dentist, contact your primary care doctor for a referral or call a local dental clinic.
